2 ideas here:

1) We make sure EVERY generated file has a discoverable name.  I don't know what this would do for things like proto files which are generated (isn't filename important in proto?) but it would work for .go files.

2) We make sure EVERY generated file has a CLEARLY documented comme

Either approach is pretty enormous.  To stick such a tag in emitted files means touching gengo and we still have the problem that the script are grepping for a string which other tools will find in those scripts.

I'm going to see if there's a less dramatic answer if we just hold our noses a bit.

Yeah, this is kind of a mess because of a lot of reasons.  Until workspaces lands, we are forced to phony-up a GOPATH.  Go's default is to put the module cache under GOPATH[0], which means it gets stored in this directory tree.

function kube::util::mktemp-dir() {

    if [[ -n "$1" ]]; then
        script="$1"
    fi
    base="$(basename "${script}")"
    mktemp -d -t "${base}.XXXXXX"
}
```

> I understood the phrase you're quoting to mean that the existing behavior is to decode {}{} as two consecutive JSON objects, not that {}{} is itself valid JSON according to the standard.
